questions.text: What is the purpose of the Git staging area?

options.text: It automatically saves all changes every five minutes
It lets you select exactly which changes to include in the next commit
It stores your code on GitHub's servers
It runs your smart contract tests before pushing

questions.explanation: The staging area (index) allows you to prepare a commit by selecting specific files or even specific chunks of changes, rather than committing everything that was modified.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

The staging area (index) allows you to prepare a commit by selecting specific files or even specific chunks of changes, rather than committing everything that was modified.

questions.text: In a blockchain project, which file must ALWAYS be in .gitignore?

options.text: .env (which contains private keys and RPC URLs)

questions.explanation: The .env file contains sensitive information like private keys. If committed to Git, it can be scraped by attackers. It must always be in .gitignore.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

The .env file contains sensitive information like private keys. If committed to Git, it can be scraped by attackers. It must always be in .gitignore.

questions.text: What is a Pull Request on GitHub?

options.text: A command that downloads the latest code from GitHub
A proposal to merge changes from one branch into another, enabling code review before integration
A request to GitHub to increase your repository's storage limit
A way to download someone else's repository without forking it

questions.explanation: A Pull Request (PR) is a GitHub feature that asks the repository maintainers to review and merge your branch changes. It enables discussion, code review, and automated tests before code enters the main branch.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

A Pull Request (PR) is a GitHub feature that asks the repository maintainers to review and merge your branch changes. It enables discussion, code review, and automated tests before code enters the main branch.

questions.text: "If I accidentally commit my private key to a public GitHub repository, I can simply delete the file and make a new commit - the key is now safe because it's no longer in the latest version of the code."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and explains that the key remains in Git history.+1 mark: Mentions that funds must be moved immediately and/or that Git history must be rewritten (not just a new commit).
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and explains that the key remains in Git history.
+1 mark: Mentions that funds must be moved immediately and/or that Git history must be rewritten (not just a new commit).
